frankish_kingdom_500.name: Frankish Kingdom
frankish_kingdom_500.description: A simplified early Frankish realm in northern Gaul and the Rhineland. Clovis I expanded Frankish power and his conversion to Catholic Christianity helped shape medieval Western Europe.
importantEvents: Clovis becomes king of the Salian Franks around 481
frankish_kingdom_500.notes: The borders shown here are simplified and should later be replaced with sourced historical GIS data.

visigothic_kingdom_500.name: Visigothic Kingdom
visigothic_kingdom_500.description: A major post-Roman kingdom ruling much of Iberia and parts of southern Gaul. Around 500 CE its center was still connected to Toulouse before later shifting toward Toledo.
importantEvents: Defeat by the Franks at Vouillé in 507
visigothic_kingdom_500.notes: The shown polygon is a simplified teaching shape, not a sourced historical boundary.

ostrogothic_kingdom_500.name: Ostrogothic Kingdom
ostrogothic_kingdom_500.description: The Ostrogothic Kingdom controlled Italy after Theoderic defeated Odoacer. It preserved many Roman administrative traditions while ruling as a Gothic kingdom.
importantEvents: Byzantine reconquest ends Ostrogothic rule in Italy
ostrogothic_kingdom_500.notes: The shown polygon is simplified and will later be replaced with sourced historical boundary data.

byzantine_empire_500.name: Byzantine Empire
byzantine_empire_500.description: The Eastern Roman Empire remained the dominant imperial power in the eastern Mediterranean. Around 500 CE it controlled Constantinople, the Balkans, Greece, Anatolia, and parts of the eastern Mediterranean.
importantEvents: Justinian's reconquests begin later in the 6th century
The empire survives until the Ottoman conquest of Constantinople in 1453
byzantine_empire_500.notes: The shown polygon is a simplified teaching shape and not an exact border reconstruction.

anglo_saxon_kingdoms_500.name: Anglo-Saxon Kingdoms
anglo_saxon_kingdoms_500.description: A simplified representation of early Anglo-Saxon polities in Britain. Around 500 CE the region was politically fragmented and still undergoing major cultural and religious change.
importantEvents: Migration and settlement in post-Roman Britain
Christianization begins later, especially from the mission of Augustine in 597
Norman conquest ends the Anglo-Saxon royal line in 1066
anglo_saxon_kingdoms_500.notes: The area shown is highly simplified and represents a broad cultural-political zone rather than a single unified kingdom.
